name: Cipactli
description: Cipactli was a primordial sea monster in Aztec mythology, often depicted as a monstrous caiman or crocodile-like creature. It possessed an insatiable hunger and a chaotic nature, embodying the primordial waters from which the world was formed. Cipactli's body was covered in mouths, each craving sustenance, making it a symbol of constant need and the destructive potential of the untamed ocean. The gods Quetzalcoatl and Tezcatlipoca sacrificed themselves to defeat Cipactli and create the world from its dismembered body. The earth itself is said to be formed from Cipactli's remains, with its scales becoming the mountains, its skin the plains, and its hair the trees. This act of creation through sacrifice highlights the Aztec belief in the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth, where destruction is necessary for new beginnings. Cipactli represents the chaotic and unpredictable forces of nature that the gods had to overcome to establish order and stability in the world.
subtitle: Primordial Caiman of Creation and Chaos
appearance: Cipactli is described as a massive, reptilian creature, resembling a caiman or crocodile but on a colossal scale. Its body is covered in numerous mouths, each filled with sharp teeth. Its skin is rough and scaly, and its eyes are said to glow with an eerie light. Some depictions show it with spines or protrusions along its back, adding to its monstrous appearance.
habitat: Cipactli existed in the primordial waters before the creation of the world. It dwelled in the vast, chaotic ocean that preceded the ordered cosmos.
behavior: Cipactli is characterized by its insatiable hunger and chaotic nature. It represents the untamed and unpredictable forces of the primordial waters. Its constant craving for sustenance symbolizes the endless cycle of destruction and renewal. Cipactli is a force of chaos that must be overcome to establish order and stability.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of the World
keyMyths.description: Quetzalcoatl and Tezcatlipoca transformed themselves into serpents and tore Cipactli apart to create the earth. Her body parts became different features of the landscape, demonstrating the gods' power and sacrifice.

sources.author: Fray Bernardino de Sahagún
sources.work: Historia General de las Cosas de Nueva España
sources.description: A comprehensive account of Aztec culture and beliefs, including descriptions of Cipactli and its role in creation myths.
sources.text: A comprehensive account of Aztec culture and beliefs, including descriptions of Cipactli and its role in creation myths.

sources.author: Miguel León-Portilla
sources.description: An analysis of Aztec philosophy and worldview, exploring the significance of Cipactli as a symbol of primordial chaos.
